turns.content: Hey, I just finished a small project using the standard library but noticed some of my code looks weird. I managed to make it compile by setting edition = 2018 in Cargo.toml, yet there are still these legacy traits and older syntax floating around that feel outdated. Is there a better way to handle this migration from 2018 to 2021 without breaking everything?

The best approach is to use the cargo fix --edition command which automatically updates your codebase for you. This tool leverages the new syntax adoption rules in Rust edition 2021 to modernize traits and remove deprecated features like boxed trait objects that are no longer needed. It systematically replaces old patterns with the cleaner idioms available in the newer standard library version.

That sounds perfect but I want to understand exactly what changes cargo will make so I can verify it doesn't break my custom types. Can you explain which specific traits and syntax rules shift when we upgrade?

When running cargo fix --edition, the tool targets specific foundational concepts like Box trait objects and deref coercion that were common in 2018 but are now discouraged or removed in 2021. It will replace those legacy patterns with explicit references and standard smart pointers to ensure your code relies on the current stable API surface rather than deprecated traits.
